commit dd659195247a1e99343cdce30b9602b3c1e7bd9d
Author: JP Kobryn (Meta) <jp.kobryn@linux.dev>

mm/mempolicy: track user-defined mempolicy allocations

Add NUMA mempolicy allocation counters as new node stat items to track
allocations to nodes and whether they were intentional or fallbacks.

> diff --git a/mm/mempolicy.c b/mm/mempolicy.c
> index e5528c35bbb8..c3bacc927a21 100644
> --- a/mm/mempolicy.c
> +++ b/mm/mempolicy.c

[ ... ]

> +static void mpol_count_numa_alloc(struct mempolicy *pol, int intended_nid,
> +				  struct page *page, unsigned int order)
> +{
> +	int actual_nid = page_to_nid(page);
> +	long nr_pages = 1L << order;
> +	bool is_hit;
> +
> +	if (!current->mempolicy)
> +		return;

If a user applies a mempolicy to a specific memory range via the mbind()
syscall without setting a process-wide policy (set_mempolicy()), will
this early return silently exclude those explicitly user-designated
mbind() allocations?

Since alloc_pages_mpol() correctly receives the user's VMA policy as the
pol parameter, should the code filter based on the pol parameter itself
(e.g., bypassing if it is the internal default_policy) rather than the
state of the executing task?

> +
> +	if (pol->mode == MPOL_BIND || pol->mode == MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY)
> +		is_hit = node_isset(actual_nid, pol->nodes);
> +	else
> +		is_hit = (actual_nid == intended_nid);
> +
> +	if (is_hit) {
> +		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(actual_nid), NUMA_MPOL_HIT, nr_pages);
> +	} else {
> +		/* account for miss on the fallback node */
> +		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(actual_nid), NUMA_MPOL_MISS, nr_pages);
> +
> +		/* account for foreign on the intended node */
> +		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(intended_nid), NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN, nr_pages);

For an MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY policy, policy_nodemask() does not override the
initial nid parameter, meaning intended_nid defaults to the local
executing node (numa_node_id()).

If the executing node is not part of the preferred nodemask, and the
allocation falls back to this executing node, does this log both a
NUMA_MPOL_MISS and a NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN on the exact same node?

Would attributing a foreign event to the executing node when it was never
intended violate the semantic definition of the stat ("allocation intended
for this node")?

> +	}
> +}
> +
>  /**
>   * alloc_pages_mpol - Allocate pages according to NUMA mempolicy.
>   * @gfp: GFP flags.

[ ... ]

> @@ -2494,6 +2537,10 @@ static struct page *alloc_pages_mpol(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order,
>  		}
>  	}
>  
> +out:
> +	if (page)
> +		mpol_count_numa_alloc(pol, nid, page, order);
> +
>  	return page;
>  }

Since user-defined mempolicies also govern allocations in other
subsystems that bypass alloc_pages_mpol() entirely, will those
allocations silently bypass the new NUMA_MPOL_* counters?

For instance, HugeTLB pages evaluate policies and allocate via
alloc_hugetlb_folio() / alloc_buddy_hugetlb_folio(), and bulk memory
allocations use paths like alloc_pages_bulk_mempolicy_noprof().

Will this lead to systematically incomplete metrics for workloads
relying on these allocators?

Target Commit:
commit dd659195247a1e99343cdce30b9602b3c1e7bd9d
Author: JP Kobryn (Meta) <jp.kobryn@linux.dev>
Date:   Tue Mar 17 05:06:57 2026 +0000

    mm/mempolicy: track user-defined mempolicy allocations
    
    When investigating pressure on a NUMA node, there is no straightforward way
    to determine which user-defined policies are driving allocations to it.
    
    Add NUMA mempolicy allocation counters as new node stat items. These
    counters track allocations to nodes and also whether the allocations were
    intentional or fallbacks.
    
    The new stats follow the existing numa hit/miss/foreign style and have the
    following meanings:
    
      hit
        - for nodemask-based policies, allocation succeeded within nodemask
        - for other policies, allocation succeeded on intended node
        - counted on the node of the allocation
      miss
        - allocation intended for other node, but happened on this one
        - counted on other node
      foreign
        - allocation intended on this node, but happened on other node
        - counted on this node
    
    The existing numa_* counters cannot be adjusted to fill this role because
    they are incremented in zone_statistics(), which also covers non-policy
    allocations such as alloc_pages_node(). The mempolicy context is not
    applicable at that level since in-kernel callers may make their own node
    decisions independent of any task policy.
    
    Allocations where task mempolicy is NULL are excluded since they do not
    reflect a user designation and are already accounted for in the existing
    numa_* stats.
    
    Counters are exposed per-node in nodeN/vmstat and globally in /proc/vmstat.
    They provide the information needed in step 3 of the investigation workflow
    below:
    
    1) Pressure/OOMs reported while system-wide memory is free.
    2) Check /proc/zoneinfo or per-node stats in .../nodeN/vmstat to narrow
       down node(s) under pressure.
    3) Check numa_mpol_{hit,miss,foreign} counters (added by this patch) on
       node(s) to see what policy is driving allocations there (and whether
       they are intentional vs fallback).
         - If active: a user-defined policy is driving allocations to the
           node. Proceed to step 4.
         - If inactive: pressure is from allocations without a user-defined
           policy. Stop and investigate task placement or node capacity
           instead.
    4) Use /proc/*/numa_maps to identify tasks using the policy.
    
    Signed-off-by: JP Kobryn (Meta) <jp.kobryn@linux.dev>

diff --git a/include/linux/mmzone.h b/include/linux/mmzone.h
index 7bd0134c241c..a9407a3b4c8a 100644
--- a/include/linux/mmzone.h
+++ b/include/linux/mmzone.h
@@ -323,6 +323,11 @@ enum node_stat_item {
 	PGSCAN_ANON,
 	PGSCAN_FILE,
 	PGREFILL,
+#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA
+	NUMA_MPOL_HIT,
+	NUMA_MPOL_MISS,
+	NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN,
+#endif
 #ifdef CONFIG_HUGETLB_PAGE
 	NR_HUGETLB,
 #endif
diff --git a/mm/mempolicy.c b/mm/mempolicy.c
index e5528c35bbb8..c3bacc927a21 100644
--- a/mm/mempolicy.c
+++ b/mm/mempolicy.c
@@ -2426,6 +2426,47 @@ static struct page *alloc_pages_preferred_many(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order,
 	return page;
 }
 
+/*
+ * Count a user-defined mempolicy allocation. Stats are tracked per-node.
+ * The following numa_mpol_{hit/miss/foreign} pattern is used:
+ *
+ *   hit
+ *     - for nodemask-based policies, allocation succeeded within nodemask
+ *     - for other policies, allocation succeeded on intended node
+ *     - counted on the node of the allocation
+ *   miss
+ *     - allocation intended for other node, but happened on this one
+ *     - counted on other node
+ *   foreign
+ *     - allocation intended for this node, but happened on other node
+ *     - counted on this node
+ */
+static void mpol_count_numa_alloc(struct mempolicy *pol, int intended_nid,
+				  struct page *page, unsigned int order)
+{
+	int actual_nid = page_to_nid(page);
+	long nr_pages = 1L << order;
+	bool is_hit;
+
+	if (!current->mempolicy)
+		return;
+
+	if (pol->mode == MPOL_BIND || pol->mode == MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY)
+		is_hit = node_isset(actual_nid, pol->nodes);
+	else
+		is_hit = (actual_nid == intended_nid);
+
+	if (is_hit) {
+		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(actual_nid), NUMA_MPOL_HIT, nr_pages);
+	} else {
+		/* account for miss on the fallback node */
+		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(actual_nid), NUMA_MPOL_MISS, nr_pages);
+
+		/* account for foreign on the intended node */
+		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(intended_nid), NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN, nr_pages);
+	}
+}
+
 /**
  * alloc_pages_mpol - Allocate pages according to NUMA mempolicy.
  * @gfp: GFP flags.
@@ -2444,8 +2485,10 @@ static struct page *alloc_pages_mpol(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order,
 
 	nodemask = policy_nodemask(gfp, pol, ilx, &nid);
 
-	if (pol->mode == MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY)
-		return alloc_pages_preferred_many(gfp, order, nid, nodemask);
+	if (pol->mode == MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY) {
+		page = alloc_pages_preferred_many(gfp, order, nid, nodemask);
+		goto out;
+	}
 
 	if (IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE) &&
 	    /* filter "hugepage" allocation, unless from alloc_pages() */
@@ -2471,7 +2514,7 @@ static struct page *alloc_pages_mpol(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order,
 				gfp | __GFP_THISNODE | __GFP_NORETRY, order,
 				nid, NULL);
 			if (page || !(gfp & __GFP_DIRECT_RECLAIM))
-				return page;
+				goto out;
 			/*
 			 * If hugepage allocations are configured to always
 			 * synchronous compact or the vma has been madvised
@@ -2494,6 +2537,10 @@ static struct page *alloc_pages_mpol(gfp_t gfp, unsigned int order,
 		}
 	}
 
+out:
+	if (page)
+		mpol_count_numa_alloc(pol, nid, page, order);
+
 	return page;
 }
 
diff --git a/mm/vmstat.c b/mm/vmstat.c
index b33097ab9bc8..4a8384441870 100644
--- a/mm/vmstat.c
+++ b/mm/vmstat.c
@@ -1291,6 +1291,11 @@ const char * const vmstat_text[] = {

 	[I(PGSCAN_FILE)]			= "pgscan_file",
 	[I(PGREFILL)]				= "pgrefill",
+#ifdef CONFIG_NUMA
+	[I(NUMA_MPOL_HIT)]			= "numa_mpol_hit",
+	[I(NUMA_MPOL_MISS)]			= "numa_mpol_miss",
+	[I(NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN)]			= "numa_mpol_foreign",
+#endif
 #ifdef CONFIG_HUGETLB_PAGE

 #endif

Okay, here's what I've got after digging into this mempolicy accounting code: It seems there are a few significant problems that need to be addressed in how we're tracking NUMA allocations with respect to user-defined mempolicies.

First, the code uses `current->mempolicy` as a key to decide whether or not to count allocations toward a mempolicy. That's a major flaw. If a user sets a VMA-specific policy using `mbind()` but *doesn't* set a task-wide policy with `set_mempolicy()`, the code just skips counting that allocation entirely. Conversely, if a task *does* have a policy set (even one that might be overridden for a specific allocation with `__GFP_THISNODE`), those allocations get counted as mempolicy-driven, even if they're not. Basically, the stats are going to be wrong because they are dependent on whether `current->mempolicy` is set, not whether the allocation's policy is user-defined. The code should really be checking the `pol` argument passed to the allocation function to determine if the allocation is policy-driven (e.g. if `pol == &default_policy`, don't count it).

Second, the way the code handles `MPOL_BIND` and `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY` policies when they *fail* to allocate on the intended nodes is deeply flawed. The intention of these policies is to allocate from a set of nodes represented by a nodemask. If the allocation fails to satisfy this, the code falls back to allocate elsewhere. Here's where it gets funky: if the allocation falls back, it then records a "foreign" event on the *local* node (`numa_node_id()`). This is only used as a fallback node. If the user set a nodemask for `MPOL_BIND` or `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY` that's distinct from the local node, it's just not right to charge a "foreign" event to the local node if the allocation failed to be satisfied on one of the *intended* nodes. This is especially bad in the case where the fallback occurs on the local node. In this case, `foreign` is set on the local node *as well as* miss on the local node. This creates completely misleading stats that say "this node missed and got a foreign allocation at the same time." This is not logically consistent with the intention of nodemask policies. The "foreign" charge should either be distributed to the nodes within the policy's nodemask, or it should not be recorded at all. The underlying concept of "foreign" is not well-defined for nodemask-based policies without a `home_node` set. The current behavior is broken.

There's also another major issue: the stats *completely* ignore bulk allocations made with a mempolicy via `alloc_pages_bulk_mpol_noprof()`. This means a large part of the memory allocation activity driven by mempolicy is just invisible to the new counters, making the stats incomplete and potentially useless. The commit message implied that the stats should track user-defined allocations, but this only applies to single-page allocations. It should be tracked with all mempolicy allocations, no matter the size or allocation function.

In summary, the use of `current->mempolicy` as the key, the mishandling of fallback cases with nodemask-based policies like `MPOL_BIND` and `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY`, and the omission of bulk allocations make the mempolicy-based NUMA accounting fundamentally unreliable.

    "thought": "**Analysis of NUMA Accounting for Memory Allocations**

Okay, so I've been tracing the memory allocation paths and how NUMA statistics are tracked, and I think I've found a subtle but significant issue, specifically in how the "foreign" allocation statistics are handled, especially for nodemask-based policies. Let's start with the basics. It seems that many core allocation paths, such as `shmem_alloc_folio`, `swap_state.c`, and several others ultimately funnel down to `alloc_pages_mpol`. This function takes a `nid` argument that *usually* comes from `numa_node_id()`, which represents the current CPU's NUMA node. However, there's the possibility, in the context of migration, where `alloc_migration_target` passes in the `folio_nid(src)`. This is usually a valid node, but I need to make sure that a folio always has a valid nid.

The crux of the problem lies in `mpol_count_numa_alloc`, the function responsible for counting the NUMA allocations. This is where we account for hits, misses, and *foreign* allocations. The "foreign" count should reflect when a memory allocation was *intended* for a specific node but ended up on a different one. However, after tracing how `intended_nid` is derived, I realize a potential flaw, particularly when `MPOL_BIND` or `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY` policies are in effect, or with an interleaved policy.

For `MPOL_BIND` and `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY`, the intent is defined by a *nodemask*, a set of nodes rather than a single node. The code checks if the actual node of allocation `actual_nid` is present in the nodemask (`pol->nodes`). If `actual_nid` is not in the nodemask, the allocation is considered "foreign", and the "foreign" count is attributed to `intended_nid`, which, critically, *remains* the original `numa_node_id()`.

Here's the problem: if the current node (`numa_node_id()`) isn't in `pol->nodes`, the code essentially claims that we intended to allocate memory on the *current* node, but it ended up elsewhere. That is not correct! The *intent* was for any node in the `pol->nodes`. It's misleading to attribute this as a mis-intention to allocate on the current node. This means we're mis-categorizing where the allocation was intended and mis-reporting the source of the `FOREIGN` stat. The current implementation is counting it on the wrong node, the node that *initiated* the allocation, and not the intended set of nodes.

So, how does this affect nodemask-based policies like `MPOL_BIND`? If, during an `MPOL_BIND` allocation, memory is actually allocated on a fallback node because of, say, cpuset constraints, the current node (`numa_node_id()`) is incorrectly credited with the "foreign" allocation. This is wrong. The user wasn't trying to allocate on `numa_node_id()`; they were trying to allocate on the nodes in `pol->nodes`. This is a significant semantic error.

Finally, I notice that in `alloc_pages_bulk`, the new statistics are NOT being used, so this does not address bulk allocations. I need to review `mm/mempolicy.c` diff more closely. This needs to be considered when addressing the issue, as this is a blind spot in the current patch.

    "thought": "**Incomplete NUMA Accounting & Potential OOB Access**

Okay, here's my breakdown of the situation. I've been digging into this patch related to NUMA memory policy accounting, and I've identified a significant gap. Initially, I thought the primary issue was just the missing accounting for bulk and HugeTLB allocations. I confirmed that `alloc_hugetlb_folio_nodemask` used for HugeTLB allocations and `alloc_pages_bulk_mempolicy_noprof` and its helpers, used for bulk allocations, are *not* calling `mpol_count_numa_alloc`, thus bypassing the accounting added by the patch. This means the claim of tracking *all* user-defined mempolicy allocations is incorrect, since a substantial portion of allocations would be missed.

But the issue is much deeper, and potentially worse, because I discovered a likely *kernel panic* situation. Here's the scenario: if a `MPOL_PREFERRED` mempolicy is rebound due to a cpuset change and the intersection of the mempolicy's node mask with the current cpuset is empty, then the `pol->nodes` mask will be cleared. With the mask cleared, the `first_node()` macro will return `MAX_NUMNODES`. That `MAX_NUMNODES` is then passed as the `nid` to several functions. Specifically, `policy_nodemask` gets this value, which then becomes the `preferred_nid` passed into `__alloc_frozen_pages_noprof`.

I saw a `WARN_ON_ONCE` in `__alloc_pages_noprof`, but that code did not prevent the issue.

The problem lies with the `node_zonelist` function. When `nid` is `MAX_NUMNODES` it will index out of bounds, because `NODE_DATA(nid)` is an out-of-bounds array access. This could occur if the mempolicy is set to `MPOL_PREFERRED` and the preferred node is no longer available in the cpuset and the patch fails to account for this. The current implementation of `__alloc_frozen_pages_noprof` does *not* handle this and will result in a kernel crash due to an out-of-bounds access. While it's great this patch is trying to improve accounting, the issue would appear to be a serious one due to this potential for out-of-bounds access.

I also double-checked the logic in `mpol_count_numa_alloc`. In that function, an `intended_nid` of `MAX_NUMNODES` would cause `mod_node_page_state` to be called with an invalid `NODE_DATA` index, resulting in out-of-bounds access. This confirmed my initial concern.

So, in summary, this patch is incomplete in accounting for all mempolicy allocations. Additionally, *existing* code (not just this patch, but pre-existing code that is exposed by the change this patch makes) can lead to a *kernel panic* condition if a preferred NUMA node becomes unavailable. We need to handle `MAX_NUMNODES` properly to prevent this crash. We might need to handle this in `first_node()` to ensure that `first_node()` returns a valid node, even if the node mask is empty.

    "thought": "**Analyzing Potential NUMA Node Out-of-Bounds Access in `alloc_migration_target_by_mpol`**

Okay, let's break this down. In `alloc_migration_target_by_mpol`, the initial `nid` is set using `numa_node_id()`. The crucial point is that if it's *not* a hugetlb folio, `policy_nodemask` is *not* called at the higher level, and thus, that initial `nid` value is preserved and assumed valid. However, `policy_nodemask` is *always* called eventually within `folio_alloc_mpol` and `alloc_pages_mpol`. This is where the problem starts to manifest.

The real question is: Can `policy_nodemask` ever change `nid` to an *invalid* value, specifically `NUMA_NO_NODE` (or the effectively out-of-bounds `MAX_NUMNODES`)?  Let's walk through the cases in `policy_nodemask`:

*   **MPOL\_PREFERRED:**  Here, `*nid` is set to `first_node(pol->nodes)`. If `pol->nodes` is empty, `first_node` returns `MAX_NUMNODES`. This is the potential problem!
*   **MPOL\_PREFERRED\_MANY, MPOL\_BIND:** These can set `*nid = pol->home_node`. If `pol->home_node` is invalid, that's another bad scenario.
*   **MPOL\_INTERLEAVE, MPOL\_WEIGHTED\_INTERLEAVE:**  These call potentially valid functions that set `nid` via `interleave_nid` and `weighted_interleave_nid`, but the underlying logic could have a flaw in it, as well.

The key flaw is when `pol->mode == MPOL_PREFERRED` and `pol->nodes` is empty. Here, `intended_nid` becomes `MAX_NUMNODES`.  The `mpol_count_numa_alloc` function is the point of access that can expose this. Within `mpol_count_numa_alloc`, the `actual_nid` (where the page actually was allocated), is valid. The condition `is_hit = (actual_nid == MAX_NUMNODES)` will *always* be false. The problem then surfaces when it does:

```c
mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(actual_nid), NUMA_MPOL_MISS, nr_pages);
mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(MAX_NUMNODES), NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN, nr_pages);
```

The second call, `NODE_DATA(MAX_NUMNODES)`, is definitely an out-of-bounds access. We are trying to index outside the array of node data.  This is a critical issue. The code attempts to account for a 'foreign' allocation to a non-existent NUMA node.

This needs fixing. The solution, at the minimum, requires validation in `policy_nodemask` of the `first_node` result. A check for empty `nodemask` needs to be made, and a fallback to a default node is necessary if the nodemask is indeed empty. And potentially changes to the logic in `mpol_count_numa_alloc` to correctly handle `MAX_NUMNODES`, or avoid using it altogether.

I'm starting by looking at the default policy, `default_policy`, which has a reference count initialized to 1, meaning it's never freed. Then, there's `preferred_node_policy[MAX_NUMNODES]`. Hmm, that looks like it's used to define node preferences.

Ah, the comment clears it up: these policies, like `default_policy` and `preferred_node_policy[]`, are statically allocated and their reference counts are never decremented to zero. Okay, looking at `numa_policy_init`, I see that `preferred_node_policy` is initialized with policies for each node, using `nodemask_of_node(nid)`. So the `preferred_node_policy` *does* have node information, which is good. I need to keep this in mind.

Now, let's circle back to `vma_alloc_folio`. I'm thinking about how `alloc_pages_preferred_many` might be called. Looking at `alloc_pages_mpol`, there's a call to `policy_nodemask`, and then depending on the policy `alloc_pages_preferred_many` might be called. Specifically, if the policy is `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY`. So, what if `policy_nodemask` somehow returns an empty `nodemask`?

It returns `-EINVAL` if the `nodes` is empty. So, that's not possible! Good.

Now, what about `mpol_rebind_preferred`? Does it modify `pol->nodes`? No, it doesn't. And what about `mpol_set_nodemask`? I need to keep digging, but this is a good start.

    "thought": "**Analyzing NUMA Policy Handling in Memory Allocation**

Alright, let's break this down. I'm looking at how NUMA policies are handled, specifically focusing on `mpol_count_numa_alloc` and its interaction with various memory allocation paths. The core of this analysis starts with `mpol_set_nodemask`, which essentially sets the NUMA policy for an object. This call, in turn, uses `mpol_ops[pol->mode].create(pol, &nsc->mask2)` to instantiate this policy.

For `MPOL_PREFERRED`, `MPOL_BIND`, `MPOL_INTERLEAVE`, and `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY`, the underlying policy creation functions, such as `mpol_new_preferred` and `mpol_new_nodemask`, all explicitly check if the node mask `pol->nodes` is empty. If it is, they immediately return `-EINVAL`. This is important. My immediate thought is, the system *guarantees* that `pol->nodes` is non-empty for these modes. They can *never* be empty. Makes sense.

But wait. What about `MPOL_LOCAL`? That doesn't use `pol->nodes` at all. Makes sense. And `MPOL_DEFAULT`? No mask for that one, obviously.

Now, I'm shifting focus to `mpol_count_numa_alloc`. This is where the core logic exists:

```c
	if (!current->mempolicy)
		return;

	if (pol->mode == MPOL_BIND || pol->mode == MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY)
		is_hit = node_isset(actual_nid, pol->nodes);
	else
		is_hit = (actual_nid == intended_nid);
```

Crucially, `pol` here isn't necessarily `current->mempolicy`. It's an *argument* passed to this function. Where does this `pol` come from? Well, `alloc_pages_mpol` gets it. The call chain leads to `vma_alloc_folio_noprof`:

```c
	pol = get_vma_policy(vma, addr, order, &ilx);
	folio = folio_alloc_mpol_noprof(gfp, order, pol, ilx, numa_node_id());
```

`get_vma_policy` can return `&default_policy` or a valid policy. Hmm. That's key. If `pol` is `&default_policy`, its mode is `MPOL_DEFAULT`. So, the `!current->mempolicy` check in `mpol_count_numa_alloc` doesn't stop us from getting here, even if `current->mempolicy` *is* set, `pol` might be the default policy. What happens if `pol` *is* `MPOL_DEFAULT`?

In that case, the `else` clause gets used: `is_hit = (actual_nid == intended_nid);`.

Now, where does `intended_nid` come from? It's passed from `alloc_pages_mpol`:

```c
	nodemask = policy_nodemask(gfp, pol, ilx, &nid);
```

If the policy is `MPOL_DEFAULT`, `policy_nodemask` returns `NULL`, and `nid` is unchanged. So, `intended_nid` *is* the original `nid` passed to `alloc_pages_mpol`. In `vma_alloc_folio_noprof`, this original `nid` is `numa_node_id()`. `numa_node_id()` returns the current CPU's node, which should always be a valid node ID (>= 0). And so, `intended_nid` is always >= 0. And therefore, `actual_nid` is also >= 0. It all seems good.

But… what if `alloc_pages_mpol` is called with `NUMA_NO_NODE` as `nid`? Is that possible? Is `alloc_pages_mpol` ever called with `NUMA_NO_NODE`? Need to check. I've already searched for calls to `alloc_pages_mpol`, `folio_alloc_mpol_noprof` and `folio_alloc_mpol`. Doesn't immediately seem to be the case, and even `numa_node_id()` doesn't seem to return that.

Are there ANY other callers to `folio_alloc_mpol`? I need to know where all this is used!

    "thought": "**Analysis of NUMA Mempolicy Accounting Logic**

Okay, so I've been digging into this `mpol_count_numa_alloc` function and I think I've spotted a potential issue related to how it handles mempolicy accounting, particularly when VMAs and task mempolicies interact. The core of the problem seems to stem from this check: `if (!current->mempolicy) return;`. This seemingly straightforward check to see if a mempolicy is set on the *task* causes a problem.

Here's my thought process:

1.  **The Context:** The function aims to account for NUMA allocations based on the policy in effect. It takes `pol` which is a pointer to the mempolicy being used for the *current* allocation, `intended_nid` which is the preferred node for the allocation, and other arguments.

2.  **The Core Logic:** The code determines if an allocation is a "hit" or a "miss" based on the `pol->mode` and then updates NUMA statistics accordingly. If the task's mempolicy is NULL the function returns.

3.  **The Problem:** The issue arises when we have a scenario where the VMA has a mempolicy set (e.g., through `mbind`), but the task itself doesn't have a mempolicy set (`current->mempolicy` is NULL, which is the default). In this case, even though a user *has* designated a mempolicy for that *specific* memory region (the VMA) it *won't* be accounted for because of the initial `if (!current->mempolicy) return;`.

4.  **Why It's a Bug:** This behavior is not only incorrect, but also inconsistent. If a user defines a mempolicy through `mbind`, it should be accounted for. The comment "Allocations where task mempolicy is NULL are excluded since they do not reflect a user designation" does not apply when there is a VMA policy.

5.  **Alternative Scenarios:**
    *   If the allocation is using the default policy (`pol == &default_policy`), it's correct to *not* account, as the existing NUMA stats would take care of it.
    *   If the task policy is set and overrides any VMA policy it's fine.

6.  **Digging Deeper** The fact that `alloc_frozen_pages_noprof` uses the task policy doesn't change anything, because if the task policy is default, and a VMA policy is being used, it isn't accounted for.

7.  **The Fix:** The author should either:
    *   check `pol == &default_policy`, so all default allocations are not counted
    *   remove the `!current->mempolicy` check
    *   Possibly do something with `get_task_policy`, if it doesn't return `current->mempolicy`.

Essentially, the current check creates an unexpected hole in accounting for allocations *driven* by VMA policies when the task has a default mempolicy.

My immediate concern is a critical flaw in how NUMA memory policy allocations are tracked, specifically within the `mpol_count_numa_alloc` function. The fundamental problem lies in the check `!current->mempolicy`, which I now recognize as incorrect and leading to both false negatives and false positives.

Firstly, I've identified a **false negative scenario** related to the use of `mbind()`. If a process has *no* task-level memory policy ( `current->mempolicy == NULL`) but has a virtual memory area (VMA) with an explicitly defined NUMA policy assigned via `mbind()`, the current implementation *ignores* this allocation. While the `pol` argument passed to `mpol_count_numa_alloc` correctly reflects the VMA's policy (retrieved through `get_vma_policy`), the early exit triggered by `!current->mempolicy` prevents accurate accounting of these explicitly defined, user-intended NUMA allocations in `NUMA_MPOL_*` stats. This seems like a significant oversight, as `mbind` is a clear user directive and this behavior violates the principle of accounting for these allocations.

Secondly, I've identified a **false positive scenario** that stems from the use of `alloc_frozen_pages_noprof` during interrupts or `__GFP_THISNODE` allocations. If a task *does* have an existing memory policy (`current->mempolicy != NULL`) and an interrupt occurs, the interrupted task's memory policy *is not* taken into account. The interrupt, or a `__GFP_THISNODE` allocation, defaults to the `&default_policy` and bypasses the task's policy. The code *incorrectly* accounts these allocations in `NUMA_MPOL_*` statistics because the check `!current->mempolicy` is false, even though the allocation used the default policy! This leads to misattribution of interrupt-related memory allocations as user-defined NUMA policy allocations.

Furthermore, I have discovered a more nuanced issue with how `MPOL_DEFAULT` policies are handled in conjunction with interrupts and `set_mempolicy()`. The current implementation counts memory allocations done by an interrupt even if they are done to the `&default_policy` as defined by the interrupted task's mempolicy. This is fundamentally wrong because interrupts should *not* be influenced by a task's mempolicy.

The root cause of all these issues is the code's dependence on `current->mempolicy` instead of the actual `pol` being used for the allocation. The correct solution would be to revise the `mpol_count_numa_alloc` check. It should base its decision on the policy being *applied*, e.g., by excluding `&default_policy` or `pol->mode == MPOL_DEFAULT`. This modification should correctly filter out uninteresting default policy allocations and prevent miscounting interrupt-related allocations.

Finally, I've confirmed that the node mask handling for various memory policy modes (e.g., `MPOL_BIND`, `MPOL_PREFERRED`) is generally correct, but the use of the `intended_nid` in `NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN` accounting for `MPOL_BIND` presents a semantic issue.     "content": "{"content":"mm/mempolicy.c-2429-/*\nmm/mempolicy.c-2430- * Count a user-defined mempolicy allocation. If, for some reason, `nid` ends up being `NUMA_NO_NODE` at this point, the `mpol_count_numa_alloc` function will be called with `intended_nid = NUMA_NO_NODE`.

Looking into the `mpol_count_numa_alloc` function:
```c
static void mpol_count_numa_alloc(struct mempolicy *pol, int intended_nid,
				  struct page *page, unsigned int order)
{
	int actual_nid = page_to_nid(page);
	long nr_pages = 1L << order;
	bool is_hit;

	if (!current->mempolicy)
		return;

	if (pol->mode == MPOL_BIND || pol->mode == MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY)
		is_hit = node_isset(actual_nid, pol->nodes);
	else

	if (is_hit) {
		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(actual_nid), NUMA_MPOL_HIT, nr_pages);

		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(actual_nid), NUMA_MPOL_MISS, nr_pages);

		/* account for foreign on the intended node */
		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(intended_nid), NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN, nr_pages);

This will, most likely, cause a kernel panic or memory corruption, which is obviously very bad!

Hmm, I recall that `mpol_new_preferred` should prevent empty nodemasks. Okay, next I should ask if `pol->nodes` could be modified after its creation, but then the nodemask is copied and fixed for the `mempolicy`.

			first_node(pol->nodes) : interleave_nid(pol, ilx);
		break;

If `pol->nodes` is actually empty under `MPOL_INTERLEAVE`? But, looking back at `mpol_new_nodemask`
```c
static int mpol_new_nodemask(struct mempolicy *pol, const nodemask_t *nodes)
{
	if (nodes_empty(*nodes))

	pol->nodes = *nodes;
	return 0;
}

    "thought": "**Summary of Concerns Regarding NUMA Memory Policy Accounting**

My analysis reveals two primary concerns regarding the recently introduced NUMA memory policy accounting changes, specifically within the `mpol_count_numa_alloc` function and related code.

First, there is a logical flaw where VMA-level memory policies are incorrectly excluded from accounting when a task lacks a task-level policy. The implementation checks `!current->mempolicy`, assuming that the absence of a task policy implies the lack of a user-defined policy. However, `mbind()` allows users to specify memory policies on specific virtual memory areas (VMAs) without setting a global task policy. This means the code *silently* disregards allocations made under such VMA-level policies. The correct approach would be to check `pol == &default_policy` to distinguish system defaults from user-defined policies. This introduces an inconsistency where VMA policies are counted only if a task also defines a task policy.

Second, a potential out-of-bounds access vulnerability exists due to a race condition when a `MPOL_PREFERRED` policy is updated concurrently. Specifically, the patch introduces a scenario where `first_node()` within the `policy_nodemask` function can return `MAX_NUMNODES` if a race with `mpol_rebind_policy` results in reading an empty nodemask. When a preferred node is selected and the nodemask is empty, this leads to an access to `NODE_DATA(MAX_NUMNODES)` in `mod_node_page_state`. This is strictly out-of-bounds, potentially leading to a kernel crash (if the race is real and can result in the codepath executing). Although the page allocator itself should crash earlier due to the empty nodemask if the race can happen, the patch introduces this out-of-bounds access in a new codepath, meaning the vulnerability can now be triggered in an added context.

The key question is the handling of `NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN` when `is_hit` is false. If `is_hit` is false, it's accounted on `intended_nid`, and I was initially worried about potential `NUMA_NO_NODE` issues. However, `intended_nid` appears to be correctly initialized and valid. I verified that there aren't calls with `NUMA_NO_NODE` in the path to `alloc_pages_mpol` and checked the possible return of `numa_node_id()`, which is always a valid node ID.

Next, I looked at how these counters are exposed. The commits mention per-node and global counters in `/proc/vmstat`. The `vmstat_text[]` array and corresponding enum in `mm/vmstat.c` are consistent with the definitions in `mmzone.h`, so there's no mismatch there.

Now, this is the crucial part. In `mm/mempolicy.c`, the logic is: increment `NUMA_MPOL_MISS` on the *actual* node and `NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN` on the *intended* node when there's a miss. It's essentially mimicking the `NUMA_MISS` and `NUMA_FOREIGN` behavior. But is attributing `NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN` to `intended_nid` always the correct thing to do?

For `MPOL_BIND` and `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY`, the `intended_nid` is actually determined by `numa_node_id()`, which is completely arbitrary and can be any node. These policies are not tied to a single node, but to a set of nodes determined by the nodemask. If the allocation falls outside this set, it's a miss. But, the code's accounting the *current node* as the source of a *foreign* allocation. It's potentially misattributed. The current node's foreign counter is incremented, even though the current node might not even be in the policy's nodemask!

Consider a scenario: a task is bound to nodes 1 and 2, but it's *executing* on node 0, and the allocation ends up on node 3. The `foreign` counter would be incremented on node 0, but this doesn't capture that the allocation was foreign to nodes 1 or 2, which are the ones the task is actually bound to. The `foreign` counter would *misrepresent* the allocation as foreign *to* the node the task is running on. Even though there is a miss from the actual intended policy.

If `is_hit` relies on the set of nodes, then `intended_nid` is not considered in determining a hit. But why record a `foreign` event on `intended_nid` for `MPOL_BIND` / `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY`? It's not logically correct, but it's just a statistical counter. Maybe this isn't a strict bug, but this might be misattributing memory policies!

Finally, I checked the concurrency aspects related to the `pol` struct and its lifetime. Using `vma_alloc_folio_noprof` shows that `pol` (the mempolicy) is protected by refcounting or `mmap_lock`. Also, I made sure that  `pol` is alive when passed to `mpol_count_numa_alloc` in `alloc_pages_mpol` . The call to `mpol_count_numa_alloc` does not hold any extra locks, but `pol` is still alive. The core problem, I see, is in how `mpol_count_numa_alloc` handles cases where there isn't a *task* policy. The code checks `!current->mempolicy`, and if that's true, it returns immediately.     "thought": "**Analysis of NUMA Memory Policy Accounting in `mpol_count_numa_alloc`**

Okay, diving into this code, my first thought was about nodemasks. It's clear that `MPOL_PREFERRED` requires a non-empty nodemask, which ensures `first_node()` always returns a valid node ID. Similarly, `MPOL_BIND`, `MPOL_INTERLEAVE`, `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY`, and `MPOL_WEIGHTED_INTERLEAVE` also mandate non-empty nodemasks, preventing empty nodemask scenarios. This means the initial checks in `mpol_new` are robust in rejecting invalid policies.

Next, I focused on the `NUMA_NO_NODE` value.  `intended_nid` can potentially be set to `NUMA_NO_NODE` during the `policy_nodemask` function when `MPOL_LOCAL` or `MPOL_DEFAULT` is set, however, the direct callers of `folio_alloc_mpol_noprof`, such as `numa_node_id()`, will result in `intended_nid` never being `NUMA_NO_NODE`.

When examining `MPOL_BIND` and `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY` specifically, the `intended_nid` can be `NUMA_NO_NODE`, in the case that `pol->home_node` is set to `NUMA_NO_NODE`, but then this will just keep the value passed by the caller, meaning it's still a valid node ID.  Therefore, `intended_nid` within `mpol_count_numa_alloc` will always be a valid node ID (0 to `MAX_NUMNODES - 1`).

Now, the core of the problem comes down to correctly accounting NUMA hits, misses, and "foreign" allocations.  The `is_hit` check logic is crucial here.  For `MPOL_BIND` and `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY`, `is_hit` becomes `true` if the actual allocation node, `actual_nid`, is within the policy's nodemask.  If not, it's a miss.  However, for `MPOL_BIND`, this logic seems conceptually sound but subtly tricky. The "intended" node for `MPOL_BIND` is the entire nodemask. Therefore, a miss is technically a miss to any of the nodes in the `pol->nodes`. When a miss occurs, "foreign" should be accounted for the "intended node", however, the code uses the caller's node, which is `numa_node_id()`.  This counting on the *current* node might be conceptually correct, as that's where the failure is occurring, even if the "intended" node is a broader set.

The commit's documentation highlights that for NUMA stats, a foreign allocation means the allocation was *intended* on a node, but it happened elsewhere, and it is counted on the intended node. In the code, the "intended" node is `numa_node_id()` (or `home_node` if set), so this matches the code's interpretation.

Regarding locking and synchronization: `mod_node_page_state` is atomic, so there are no data races there. We have the normal RCU concerns, but `pol` is refcounted and safely accessed in this function, and `page` is a local variable. The lock used in the numa stats, via `__count_numa_event`, is done via a preempt disable/enable pair. There are no lock-ordering issues here, everything is properly accounted for, and there are no data races.

However, a key logic flaw is the check `if (!current->mempolicy) return;`. It appears this function is counting the statistics on the *task's* mempolicy instead of the actual mempolicy `pol`. If a task has no mempolicy set (i.e., `current->mempolicy == NULL`) but uses `mbind` to set a VMA policy, this code skips counting those allocations. Therefore, we may have a logic bug when VMA policies are not recorded properly, even though no synchronization issues arise.

In conclusion, although the locking and synchronization aspects look safe, there might be a logical flaw in how VMA policy allocations are counted when the task doesn't have a direct mempolicy set.

    "thought": "**Summary of NUMA Policy Analysis**

Okay, so I've been tracing how the kernel handles NUMA memory allocation policies, particularly looking for potential issues, especially those related to context switching or data corruption. Here’s what I've found, broken down into key concerns:

*   **`mpol_new_preferred` and Node Removal:** The code seems to correctly handle the case where a node might be taken offline or removed after a policy is set. The check using `NODE_DATA(intended_nid)` appears safe, as `NODE_DATA` accesses are within array bounds (`MAX_NUMNODES`), and the pointer itself could be NULL if the node was never online. Importantly, the code *doesn't* free `NODE_DATA` if a node goes offline – a good thing.

*   **Foreign Node Accounting (`NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN`):** The code attempts to track NUMA locality. For `MPOL_BIND` and `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY`, it checks if the allocated page is "hit" using `node_isset(actual_nid, pol->nodes)`. If not, it increments `NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN` on the `intended_nid`. The `intended_nid` is generally the node requested by the caller, which `alloc_pages_mpol` gets from a caller function. I’ve verified that `intended_nid` is a valid node ID.

*   **Interrupt Context Vulnerability:** **This is a critical issue.** The `mpol_count_numa_alloc` function, which tracks NUMA stats, has a serious bug. It checks `if (!current->mempolicy)` to determine whether the *current* task has a mempolicy. However, `mpol_count_numa_alloc` is called during memory allocation in interrupt context. In interrupt context, `current` is the interrupted task, which could have a mempolicy. This means that if an interrupt handler happens to be running on behalf of a process with a non-NULL mempolicy, any allocation within that interrupt handler will be incorrectly attributed to that process's mempolicy! Even worse, allocations with flags like `__GFP_THISNODE` also trigger the same issue. The code needs to check `if (pol == &default_policy)` rather than `if (!current->mempolicy)` to correctly identify user-designated mempolicy usage. This leads to *severe* inaccuracy in NUMA stat collection.

*   **Context Pollution and Shared Data Protection:** This is a clear case of context pollution. Reading `current->mempolicy` inside an interrupt handler is semantically meaningless. It's safe in the sense that it won't cause a crash (no dereferencing), but it’s still fundamentally wrong and can lead to incorrect behavior.

*   **Default Policy and Invalid Node ID:** The `default_policy` is used when no other policy is set (like in interrupts). The `default_policy`'s nodemask is uninitialized, meaning `first_node(default_policy.nodes)` returns `MAX_NUMNODES`. When `MPOL_PREFERRED` is used with this, `*nid` is set to `MAX_NUMNODES`. This `nid` value is then passed into `__alloc_frozen_pages_noprof` which then could cause a crash. The use of this uninitialized nodemask is an issue.

In summary, the most pressing issue is the interrupt context bug in `mpol_count_numa_alloc`. This should be reported. The second is that calling `first_node` on an uninitialized nodemask leads to the allocation of memory from node id `MAX_NUMNODES` which could cause a crash.

struct page *alloc_frozen_pages_noprof(gfp_t gfp, unsigned order)

Okay, so I've been tracing the logic around `alloc_pages_mpol` and the NUMA memory policy accounting. I'm seeing a potential flaw in how we determine if an allocation should be counted as user-defined, specifically within `mpol_count_numa_alloc`. The goal here is to track allocations driven by user-defined memory policies, but the current implementation appears to be missing some critical distinctions.

My initial thought was, the calls to `alloc_pages_mpol` are localized and receive valid node IDs. That's fine. The concern really revolves around the conditional logic in `mpol_count_numa_alloc`: the key lies in understanding how the `pol` argument interacts with `current->mempolicy`. The code explicitly checks `!current->mempolicy`, but I'm realizing this check is inadequate.

Here's the problem I've identified: the code implicitly assumes that if a task lacks a `current->mempolicy`, there's no user-defined policy involved. However, this isn't necessarily true. A task might have a VMA (Virtual Memory Area) with a user-defined policy, even if the task itself doesn't have a policy set. This is most likely caused by `mbind()`. If `current->mempolicy` is NULL, then the code *ignores* the VMA policy. This means mbind'd memory is NOT being accounted correctly.

The code's structure also introduces another flaw. Allocations using the `&default_policy` *should not* be counted as user-defined. But, the code checks `!current->mempolicy`, thus the code incorrectly *includes* `&default_policy` allocations when `current->mempolicy` is set, but the allocation is using the default policy (such as `__GFP_THISNODE` or from an interrupt context). In these scenarios, the accounting will erroneously increment `NUMA_MPOL_MISS` and `NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN` even when the allocations aren't driven by explicit user intent.

In short, the existing conditional logic in `mpol_count_numa_alloc` has two main problems:
1.  **Ignores VMA Policies:** It fails to account for memory allocations influenced by user-defined VMA policies when the task itself has no mempolicy.
2.  **Incorrectly Includes `&default_policy` Allocations:** Counts allocations using `&default_policy` as user-defined if the task *has* a mempolicy set, which is incorrect.

The fix is straightforward. The code should check if `pol == &default_policy`, or `pol->mode == MPOL_DEFAULT` instead of `!current->mempolicy`. This would correctly exclude default policies and accurately reflect which allocations are driven by explicit user-defined policies, including VMA policies, and exclude default policies (such as those from an interrupt or with `__GFP_THISNODE`). This requires proper accounting for allocations made via `mbind()`.

    "thought": "**Analysis of NUMA Mempolicy Accounting Logic Error**

Okay, so I've been digging into this NUMA mempolicy accounting code, and I've found a critical logic flaw. I'm focusing on how NUMA allocation hits, misses, and foreign node allocations are tracked.

It starts with this `default_policy`, which is initialized to `MPOL_LOCAL`. Now, if allocations happen in interrupt contexts, the `alloc_frozen_pages_noprof` function uses this `default_policy` because we are not in process context, by design.

The problem lies within `mpol_count_numa_alloc`. The code checks `if (!current->mempolicy) return;`. It's this conditional that's causing issues. This test checks the *task's* mempolicy, not the policy associated with the allocation. This means that if an interrupt happens while a task with an *active mempolicy* is running, the interrupt's allocations, using `&default_policy`, *will* be accounted as if they were mempolicy allocations. This is because `current->mempolicy` (from the *interrupted* task) is non-NULL.

This is a serious problem because it pollutes the statistics. The accounting is meant to track *user-defined* mempolicy allocations, but it's now also counting default policy allocations that *happen to occur* during a mempolicy-enabled task. It's an erroneous conflation of unrelated contexts.

I then started wondering, what if it's a VMA-level mempolicy set via `mbind`? Well, it's just as bad! The check in `mpol_count_numa_alloc` completely ignores the VMA's mempolicy unless the task also has its own task-level mempolicy. This essentially makes VMA-level policies invisible for the purpose of this accounting function if the process hasn't set an explicit task-level mempolicy, which defeats the whole point of `mbind`.

Looking a little more, I think the `if (!current->mempolicy)` is definitely wrong. It should probably be something like `if (pol == &default_policy)` or `if (pol->mode == MPOL_DEFAULT || pol->mode == MPOL_LOCAL)`. But the user *can* set `MPOL_LOCAL` explicitly, so, should that be counted?

I'm now thinking about potential security implications. Is there any information leakage or out-of-bounds access? If `pol->mode` is `MPOL_BIND` or `MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY`, `node_isset()` is called, and this does not happen for `default_policy`. Then I looked into whether the `intended_nid` variable is valid and whether the logic may cause out-of-bounds writes. It seems that `intended_nid` is assigned in a way that, under some mempolicy configurations, could be invalid, which would lead to an unhandled exception. This needs further verification.

Okay, let me break this down. My initial focus was on how `MPOL_LOCAL` interacts with memory allocation within the NUMA framework. I started by tracing the call paths from `do_mbind` and `do_set_mempolicy`, both of which call `mpol_new`. The core logic seemed to hinge on the behavior of `mpol_new`, particularly when handling an empty nodemask with `MPOL_PREFERRED` which it converts to `MPOL_LOCAL`. This already creates a `pol` with mode `MPOL_LOCAL`. Further the `mpol_new` function handles `MPOL_LOCAL` with empty nodemask correctly.

The critical point is that `mpol_set_nodemask` does *nothing* to set the nodemask when `mode` is already `MPOL_LOCAL`. The nodemask for a `MPOL_LOCAL` policy remains uninitialized, seemingly implying an empty nodemask. When an allocation occurs with an `MPOL_LOCAL` policy, `alloc_pages_mpol` is called.

Inside `alloc_pages_mpol`, the `policy_nodemask` function is called, but it *doesn't* contain a case for `MPOL_LOCAL`. Consequently, `policy_nodemask` returns `NULL`. The input `nid` is unchanged from its caller, which is `numa_node_id()`.

Then, I focused on `mpol_count_numa_alloc` where the analysis grew interesting. This function determines whether to count a "hit" or a "miss" based on the `pol->mode` and the `intended_nid`. With `MPOL_LOCAL`, it simply checks if `actual_nid` (the node the page *actually* was allocated on) matches `intended_nid` (which is again `numa_node_id()`).

Now, the potential for issues emerged. Since `mpol_count_numa_alloc` doesn't differentiate between a user-defined `MPOL_LOCAL` policy and the implicit `&default_policy`, a context switch to an interrupt (which uses `&default_policy` for its allocations) could, in principle, affect NUMA statistics intended for the user process. However, the stats will still be correct and accounted for on a valid node. The main concern, thus, is stat pollution, not memory corruption. So, no out of bounds access.

Finally, I considered scenarios where `intended_nid` might be `NUMA_NO_NODE`. Tracing the call stack of `alloc_pages_mpol`, revealed that callers of `alloc_pages_mpol` are `folio_alloc_mpol_noprof` and `alloc_frozen_pages_noprof` and these always pass `numa_node_id()`, so it never is `NUMA_NO_NODE`. Within `policy_nodemask`, the only potential for modification of `nid` is with `MPOL_BIND` and `MPOL_INTERLEAVE`, but I verified that neither can result in `NUMA_NO_NODE` as output. So, it's clear that `nid` remains a valid node ID throughout the allocation process.

Essentially, the system behaves correctly but statistical results might be misleading.

				  struct page *page, unsigned int order)
{
	int actual_nid = page_to_nid(page);
	long nr_pages = 1L << order;
	bool is_hit;

		/* account for foreign on the intended node */
		mod_node_page_state(NODE_DATA(intended_nid), NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN, nr_pages);

}

The code checks for node validity upfront. Specifically, `home_node` is validated early on, and since `intended_nid` relies on this, we're assured that `NODE_DATA(intended_nid)` will be valid.

Now, looking at the updates using `mod_node_page_state`, the function relies on the `actual_nid` (page's node) being valid, which seems safe given that allocated pages must reside on a valid node. The `intended_nid` (policy's node) is also derived from valid sources, so its `NODE_DATA()` is also safe.

The interesting case to consider is when the `actual_nid` and `intended_nid` are identical, and the allocation *misses*. This triggers `NUMA_MPOL_MISS` and `NUMA_MPOL_FOREIGN` on the same node. It's odd, functionally, but technically, this is not an out-of-bounds access.

My core concern centers around the flawed logic in `mpol_count_numa_alloc`. This function *intentionally* ignores VMA-level mempolicies if the task lacks a *task-level* mempolicy, which means the code is *intentionally* broken. The author's stated reasoning ("Allocations where task mempolicy is NULL are excluded...") is incorrect, as this neglects a user's explicit VMA mempolicy set via `mbind()`. This is clearly a functional bug. I believe they incorrectly assumed that a `NULL` task policy means the absence of an active policy, which isn't true when VMA policies are present. Furthermore, the inclusion of `&default_policy` allocations where the task does have a task-level policy, is incorrect.

While a missed accounting bug is not always a *security* concern, it is a significant functional defect and may also skew numa system metrics.

Diving deeper, the code accesses `pol->nodes` with a conditional based on `pol->mode`, which looks correct. Based on the checks I see, there should be no concurrent `pol` frees. Specifically, in the VMA allocation paths, we have a clear `mpol_cond_put(pol)` after the allocation. We are sure that `pol` is valid and not freed at the time `alloc_pages_mpol` is called.

Regarding potential integer overflows, a shift left by `order` (up to `MAX_PAGE_ORDER`) is fine. `intended_nid` should also be valid since it always derives from non-empty nodemasks or valid node IDs.

Specifically, there *should* be no case where `pol->nodes` can be empty with an `MPOL_PREFERRED` mode. The logic prevents this. The only thing I can see is a call to `alloc_pages_mpol` being passed `NUMA_NO_NODE`. However, I can't see this being passed. This seems very safe.

Therefore, the core issue is the flawed accounting logic in `mpol_count_numa_alloc`, resulting in an incorrect accounting of VMA mempolicies and task-level default policies under certain conditions. This is the issue I want to address.
